data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Complexity mismatch between palace administrative capacity and accelerating military-technological change produced recursive legitimation crises", "mechanism": "Ironworking diffusion from Anatolia/Caucasus after 1300 BC lowered barrier to arming non-palace actors (peasant levies, mercenaries, raiders); palace economies dependent on bronze monopolies and chariot aristocracies faced spiraling security costs; Hittite and Mycenaean elites could not tax iron or mobilize iron-armed forces without undermining their own social base; Ugarit and Levantine ports were destroyed by actors they had armed; Egypt survived because its military organization (archer-based, riverine, temple-subsidized) was less chariot-aristocracy dependent and could absorb iron transition gradually", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Sea Peoples phenomenon", "Egyptian survival"], "excludes": ["Neo-Hittite successor states", "Assyrian iron use", "Greek Dark Age social organization"] }, "forbidden": [{"case": "Iron weapons predominate in destruction layers at Mycenaean/Hittite sites before 1100 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian 20th Dynasty military remained chariot-aristocracy dependent with no iron transition evidence", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Ugarit destruction layers show exclusively bronze weaponry and no evidence of internal armed factions", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Inverts material causation: not iron shortage but iron abundance destabilized. Connects to Marxist-style contradictions between forces and relations of production, but via institutional economics."}
